g.data-narration: This topic covers graph isomorphism, sub-graphs, walks, paths, and circuits essential for understanding graph connectivity, structure, and traversal properties in discrete mathematics.
g.text: Isomorphism, Sub-graphs, Walks, Paths and Circuits

g.data-narration: Two graphs are isomorphic when a one-to-one mapping preserves adjacency. They must have matching vertices, edges, degree sequences, and structural properties for equivalence.
g.text: 1. Graph Isomorphism
Two graphs G₁≅G₂ have a bijective function f: V₁→V₂ preserving adjacency.
Properties: Reflexive, Symmetric, Transitive
Example: Both have 4 vertices, 5 edges, degree sequence 3,2,3,2

g.data-narration: A sub-graph H has vertices V'⊆V and edges E'⊆E. Spanning sub-graphs include all vertices while induced sub-graphs include edges between selected vertices.
g.text: 2. Types of Sub-graphs
Spanning Sub-graph: V(H)=V(G), all vertices, subset of edges
Induced Sub-graph: G[V'] has vertices V'⊆V and ALL edges between vertices in V'
Edge Sub-graph: Contains subset of edges and vertices incident to those edges

g.data-narration: A walk is a sequence of vertices and edges. Trails have distinct edges. Open walks start and end at different vertices, closed walks return to the start vertex.
g.text: 3. Walks
Walk: Sequence v₀e₁v₁e₂v₂...e_kv_k where each e_i connects v_{i-1} and v_i
Length: Number of edges in the walk
Open Walk: v₀≠v_k (different start/end) | Closed Walk: v₀=v_k (returns to start)
Trail: Walk with all edges distinct

g.data-narration: A path has distinct vertices and edges, never repeating vertices. Maximum path length is n-1 for n vertices. Every path is a trail, but not vice versa always.
g.text: 4. Paths
Path: Walk with all vertices distinct (consequently all edges distinct)
Every path is always a trail | In simple graphs, every trail is a path
Maximum length = |V| - 1 edges | Hamiltonian: visits every vertex once
Example: a-b-c-d-e is a path of length 4 with 5 distinct vertices

g.data-narration: A circuit is a closed trail returning to start without repeating edges. A cycle is simpler—all vertices except start and end are distinct. C₃ is the smallest cycle.
g.text: 5. Circuits and Cycles
Circuit: Closed trail, starts/ends same vertex, no edge repeated (vertices may repeat)
Cycle: Circuit where all vertices except start/end are distinct (simple cycle)
Cycle notation: Cₙ has n vertices and n edges | Smallest cycle is C₃ (triangle)
Eulerian Circuit: Uses every edge once; requires all vertices have even degree

g.data-narration: Key differences: path vs cycle differ in vertex repetition. Walk may repeat both. Every path is a trail, every cycle is a circuit. C₃ triangle is smallest.
g.text: 6. Summary: Key Relationships
Hierarchy: Path ⊂ Trail ⊂ Walk | Cycle ⊂ Circuit ⊂ Closed Walk
Handshaking Lemma: Σdeg(v) = 2|E| | Isomorphic graphs: same vertex count, edges, degree sequences
Induced sub-graphs: ALL edges between V' | Spanning: all vertices, some edges
